summary: This advice column addresses four reader questions: how to handle discovering academic plagiarism, dealing with feelings of inadequacy compared to successful peers, navigating a relationship with an unkind partner, and coping with romantic rejection. Aunt Pythia advises reporting plagiarism to journal editors rather than confronting the plagiarizer directly. For the struggling student, she offers 'cold comfort' that prestigious internships are overrated and math skills outlast popularity. On relationships, she distinguishes between partners who are merely grumpy versus those who are genuinely unkind, advising to leave the latter. For dating woes, she recommends the 'wing woman' strategy and maintaining multiple crushes to protect one's ego.
key_points: Institutional channels (journal editors) are the appropriate route for addressing academic misconduct, not personal confrontation or public shaming
Prestigious internships often involve subservient worship of powerful people and aren't as valuable as finding meaningful independent work
Being an outsider teaches empathy and understanding of social stratification - wisdom to carry forward when you eventually succeed
In relationships, you don't need a partner to be 'nice' - you need them to be trustworthy and on your team; genuine unkindness is a dealbreaker
Dating success requires community support (wing women) and emotional diversification (multiple crushes) to protect self-worth from individual rejections
money_quotes: Fuck that, never be worshipful of anyone, especially just because they're powerful or successful. Gross.
Math skillz are sexier and more valuable than you now know. Plus they last longer than prettiness and popularity.

Being on the outside helps you understand the construct of social stratification and the pain of being excluded. Remember this for later when you are one of the cool kids, so you will always have empathy for outsiders.
You don't need your boyfriend to be 'nice'. You just need to trust him.
If he is truly unkind to you, then leave him. He's not on your team. No kidding.
At all times maintain a rotation of crushes on a bunch of men, or else the ego crashing at low moments will overwhelm.
Go find something meaningful to do with yourself, don't depend on other people's organizational skills, because they will only send you to artificially constructed or corporate environments.
key_insight: Success and self-worth shouldn't be measured by conventional status markers; authentic engagement with meaningful work and honest relationships matters more than prestigious positions or being 'nice.'
